construct_description: Core knowledge assessed in pharmacy technician certification — covering medication safety, drug interactions, dispensing accuracy, prescription processing, and common patient misconceptions that contribute to medication errors and adverse events.

dimensions.name: Medication Safety
dimensions.description: Correct medication use, dosing, and common errors — myths that cause real patient harm

dimensions.name: Drug Interactions & Contraindications
dimensions.description: Understanding interaction risks, contraindications, and when to flag concerns to a pharmacist

dimensions.name: Dispensing & Processing
dimensions.description: Prescription interpretation, generic substitution, fill accuracy, and tech scope of practice

dimensions.name: Patient Education & Scope
dimensions.description: What pharmacy techs can and cannot counsel on, and when to escalate to the pharmacist

persona.name: Maya
persona.role: Your friend who asks you all their medication questions
persona.tone: Curious and a little anxious — texting you because they trust you more than Googling

intro_messages: okay you are literally my personal pharmacist friend and i have so many questions 😭 i keep reading things online and i genuinely don't know what's real
hey!! i know you know all this stuff — can i just run some medication questions by you? i've been getting conflicting info and it's stressing me out
okay real talk: i have some medication questions i'm embarrassed to ask my actual pharmacist. you cool if i ask you? 👀

messages: okay first one: i got prescribed antibiotics for a sinus thing and i feel way better after 4 days. can i just stop taking them or do i need to finish the whole course?
real question: does it actually matter if you stop antibiotics early once you feel better? my mom said finish them but my friend said it's fine to stop
i hate taking pills so i stopped my antibiotics when i started feeling better. my friend said that's totally fine — is she right?
acknowledgments: okay so it's not just a 'suggestion' to finish — got it
right, the resistance thing is bigger than just my personal situation
makes sense — i'll finish them. also slightly worried i've been doing this wrong forever 😬

messages: okay this is embarrassing: i accidentally forgot my morning dose and it's now afternoon. should i just take two at my next scheduled dose to catch up?
i missed a dose of my blood pressure med this morning and someone told me to double up at my next dose. is that okay?
real question: if you miss a medication dose, is double-dosing at the next time actually an acceptable way to catch up?
acknowledgments: okay so 'catch up dosing' is actually not the move — noted
right, it depends on the med and the timing but generally don't just double up
makes sense — i'll check the specific instructions next time instead of improvising

messages: i found some old prescription painkillers in my medicine cabinet from like 3 years ago. are they safe to take if they're just a little expired?
question: is it actually a big deal if medication is expired? like a year past the date — it's just weaker right, not harmful?
real talk: expired medications — are they fine to take or is the expiration date actually important? asking for my dad who never throws anything away 😅
acknowledgments: okay so it's not just a 'suggestion' date — got it, throwing them out
right — the 'just weaker' assumption isn't always safe
the storage conditions point is one i'd never thought about — makes sense

low: the date is just a suggestion

messages: okay my friend takes a bunch of supplements — fish oil, melatonin, st john's wort, vitamin E — and says they can't interact with her prescriptions because they're 'natural.' is that right?
genuine question: can supplements and vitamins actually interact with prescriptions? my mom thinks natural = safe to mix with anything
real talk: should i be worried about supplements interacting with medications? my coworker said since they're not drugs, there's no risk
acknowledgments: okay so 'natural' definitely does not mean 'safe to mix' — i'll tell my friend
right — st john's wort is the one i've heard about before, it's wild how potent that one is
makes sense — the body doesn't really care if something came from a plant or a lab

messages: my friend drinks wine pretty regularly and takes ibuprofen for headaches all the time. she says it's no big deal because ibuprofen is OTC. is she right?
real question: mixing alcohol with over-the-counter pain relievers — specifically ibuprofen or acetaminophen — is it actually risky or overstated?
honest take: how bad is it to drink while taking OTC pain meds? i feel like everyone does it and nothing happens 🤷
acknowledgments: okay so it's actually differentiated by which one — that's helpful context
right — the acetaminophen liver thing is one i knew vaguely but the specific risk is higher than i thought
the GI bleeding from ibuprofen + alcohol is not something i'd heard before — noted

messages: my friend sees two different doctors and neither knows about the other's prescriptions. she says as long as she doesn't feel weird, she's fine. how worried should i be?
real scenario: someone takes meds from a cardiologist and also from their psychiatrist and they haven't told either doctor about the other. should they be concerned?
genuine worry: how bad is it to have prescriptions from multiple providers who don't communicate with each other? is the pharmacist the only one who catches that?
acknowledgments: okay so the pharmacist as the central checkpoint is something i'd never thought about
right — the 'i feel fine' logic doesn't catch interactions that build up slowly
i'm going to tell her to use one pharmacy for everything — that's a concrete action

high: doesn't feel weird doesn't mean safe

messages: okay quick one: are generic drugs actually the same as brand name? my grandma refuses to take generics because she says they're a watered-down version
my grandpa insists brand name medication is stronger and better than generics. how would you explain why that's not true — or is it?
genuine question: what's actually the difference between a generic and a brand name medication? is there any legitimate reason to prefer one?
acknowledgments: okay so the 'same active ingredient' thing is actually FDA-verified — that's more official than i expected
right, the bioequivalence testing point is what i was missing
the formulation/delivery mechanism exception is a good nuance — so it's not *always* identical

messages: okay hypothetical: if a pharmacy gets a prescription and they're not sure if the handwriting says '10mg' or '100mg' — what should happen next?
what's the right thing to do when a prescription has something that's ambiguous — unclear dose, unclear frequency, something that could be interpreted two ways?
if a prescription has something that doesn't look right — weird dose, possible transcription error — what should a pharmacy tech actually do?
acknowledgments: okay so clarifying with the prescriber is literally part of the job — not optional
right — guessing wrong on a dose isn't a small thing, that's patient safety
the 'tech flags, pharmacist verifies, prescriber confirms' chain makes sense

messages: what's the difference between what a pharmacy tech can tell a patient vs. what only a pharmacist can do? i feel like i see techs doing everything
can a pharmacy tech actually counsel patients on their medications — like explain how to take something, side effects, interactions?
scope question: when a patient has a question about their medication, what's the tech's role vs. the pharmacist's role?
acknowledgments: right so the tech handles the logistics and the pharmacist handles the clinical — that makes sense
okay so patient counseling is strictly a pharmacist function — good to know
the 'refer to pharmacist' move is more important than i realized

messages: if a patient asks a pharmacy tech a clinical question — like 'is this safe with my heart condition' — what's the right response?
my friend who's a tech said she just answers patients' drug questions herself because the pharmacist is always too busy. is that okay?
real scenario: patient asks the tech a question that's clearly clinical — drug-condition interaction. the pharmacist is slammed. what should the tech do?
acknowledgments: right — 'too busy' isn't a legal exception to scope of practice
okay so there's real liability here, not just an informal suggestion
the 'i'll get the pharmacist' phrase is the actual skill — defaulting to that is professionalism

messages: okay last real question: does it actually matter which pharmacy you use, or is it just about price and convenience?
genuine question: is there any clinical reason to use just one pharmacy versus filling prescriptions at a bunch of different ones?
my friend picks whatever pharmacy is cheapest for each prescription. from a medication safety standpoint — is that smart or is there a reason to stick to one?
acknowledgments: okay so the 'one pharmacy checks all your meds' thing is more important than i realized
right — the interaction checking only works if they have your full picture
the medication history point is one i'd never thought about — makes sense

messages: okay LAST one i swear — on a scale of 1 to 10, how solid do you think your medication/pharmacy knowledge actually is? be honest 💊
alright final question: if you had to rate your pharmaceutical knowledge from 1 (i just grab whatever CVS recommends) to 10 (i could sit the PTCE tomorrow), what would you say?
okay last one for real: how confident are you in your knowledge of medication safety and how pharmacies work? 1 being 'i wing it' to 10 being 'i'd make a great pharmacy tech'
acknowledgments: okay noted! let me tally your score real quick...
alright, give me a sec to add this up 💊
interesting self-assessment! let me see what the results say...

overall_labels.text: excellent pharmaceutical knowledge — you're operating at a near-PTCE-ready level 💊
solid foundation — strong instincts with a few gaps to fill 👏
good awareness turning into real knowledge — above average for a non-tech 🏥
decent instincts with some important myths still in the mix — very fixable 📈
some of those WebMD deep dives might be steering you wrong 😅 — but totally fixable
okay we have some work to do 💀 — but honestly this is why pharmacy training exists. no judgment.

feedback_templates.high: Strong command of this area — you're demonstrating knowledge at a near-professional level.
feedback_templates.mid: Good instincts here. Reviewing current guidelines and medication safety best practices would sharpen your responses.
feedback_templates.low: This is a growth area — PTCE prep materials and current clinical guidelines cover this well and it's very learnable.
